title: Platform Selection & Module Synthesis: From Framework to Full Mental Model
description: Apply the structured four-step selection framework to real user scenarios, then synthesize all Module 4 learning into a complete mental model of the cyberdelic landscape.
framerDescription: This lesson closes Module 4 by giving you a decision-making framework and testing whether the full landscape is now yours to navigate.

sections.title: The Systematic Approach
sections.content:
You've now toured the entire current cyberdelic landscape - platforms, clinical applications, biofeedback tools, social VR, specialized categories, and hardware. What you need now is the framework that ties it all together: a systematic way to go from a person's needs to a specific, justified recommendation.

This isn't a rigid algorithm. It's a four-step thinking process that you'll internalize and eventually stop consciously running - the same way an experienced therapist doesn't consciously run through an intake checklist every session. Right now, we make it explicit so it becomes second nature.

sections.title: Step 1 - Identify Goal
sections.content:
The first question is always: what outcome is this person seeking? Not 'what do they think they want' - what result would actually serve them?

  • Daily meditation/mindfulness -> GMVR or TRIPP Calm
  • Therapeutic work (PTSD, phobias, clinical anxiety) -> Psious/Limbix/Oxford VR + licensed professional
  • Pain management -> RelieVRx or XRHealth (clinical), Flowly (biofeedback self-directed)
  • Deep transformation/mystical experience -> Maloka with facilitator, or Isness-D if research access available
  • Breathwork practice -> Othership (community), or integrate into other platforms
  • Music/sound journey -> Soundself (technodelic), Wavepaths (therapeutic music)
  • Fitness/flow state -> Supernatural or FitXR with contemplative intent
  • Creativity as practice -> Open Brush
  • Social/community connection -> VRChat (open), Sacred VR (vetted), Innerworld (peer mental health)
  • Experimental/high-intensity -> Assess readiness carefully, then Maloka or experimental platforms with preparation

The nuance: Sometimes the stated goal and the actual need differ. Someone saying 'I want a death simulation' might actually need grief therapy. Someone asking for 'the most intense VR experience' might actually need to start with basic meditation. Your job is discerning the underlying need, not just matching stated requests to platforms.

sections.title: Step 2 - Consider User
sections.content:
Once you know the goal, assess who this person is and what they bring:

Experience Level:

  • Beginner to consciousness work -> Start accessible (GMVR, TRIPP), build foundation before intensity
  • Experienced meditator/contemplative -> Can handle depth immediately (Maloka, Isness-D, experimental)
  • Psychedelic background -> May seek intensity but still needs VR-specific orientation

Clinical Vulnerability:

  • Stable, no acute conditions -> Full range available
  • Active mental health challenges -> Clinical platforms with professional oversight required
  • Trauma history -> Preparation and facilitation non-negotiable for high-intensity work
  • Contraindications (psychosis, severe dissociation, acute crisis) -> Refer to professional care, not VR

Tech Comfort:

  • VR-savvy -> Can navigate complex platforms (Resonite, VRChat)
  • Basic tech comfort -> Mainstream platforms (Quest ecosystem apps)
  • Tech-anxious -> Need hands-on setup support, simplest interfaces (GMVR free tier)

Budget:

  • Limited/fixed income -> Free tiers (GMVR), affordable options (Nature Treks $10 one-time), community support (Innerworld)
  • Moderate budget -> Standard subscriptions ($10-20/month)
  • Can invest -> Premium platforms (Supernatural $20/month), facilitated sessions (Maloka $150-300)

Values/Privacy:

  • No concerns about data collection -> Quest ecosystem is optimal
  • Privacy-conscious -> Pico hardware, open-source platforms where possible
  • Specific ethical constraints -> Honor them, find alternatives

The principle: Matching platform to person requires seeing the whole person, not just the presenting request.

sections.title: Step 3 - Match Context
sections.content:
Where and how will this practice happen? Context shapes what's viable:

Setting:

  • Home solo practice -> Standalone platforms, self-directed content
  • Clinical setting -> Professional platforms (Psious, Limbix), therapist-administered
  • Group/community -> Social VR (Sacred VR, Innerworld), or synchronized solo (Othership groups)

Support Structures Available:

  • Has therapist -> Can integrate clinical VR as adjunct to therapy
  • No professional support -> Wellness/growth platforms only, refer for clinical needs
  • Has facilitator access (you or others) -> Can engage deeper work with appropriate container
  • Isolated, no support -> Start gentle, build capacity, avoid high-intensity without backup

Facilitator Required vs. Appropriate:

  • Some platforms require facilitation (Maloka - not designed for solo)
  • Some benefit from facilitation but work solo (TRIPP, GMVR - better with guidance but functional alone)
  • Some are explicitly self-directed (Nature Treks, Open Brush)

Physical Space:

  • Small apartment -> Seated/stationary experiences work, movement VR may not
  • Dedicated VR space -> Full range including active movement
  • Institutional setting -> Consider noise, privacy, multiple users

Frequency/Duration:

  • Daily short practice -> Low-friction platforms, easy setup
  • Weekly deep sessions -> Can tolerate more setup complexity for higher quality
  • One-time/occasional -> Guided facilitated sessions may be better than self-directed subscription

The reality check: The perfect platform that doesn't fit someone's actual life circumstances won't get used. Recommendations must be practically viable.

sections.title: Step 4 - Trial & Iterate
sections.content:
The selection framework isn't 'choose once perfectly.' It's 'start well, then refine based on experience':

  • Use Free Trials (7-14 days typical): Most platforms offer trials. Use them. Don't commit to annual subscriptions before testing.
  • Try 2-3 Options When Unclear: If you're genuinely torn between TRIPP and GMVR, try both. The trial period reveals which resonates.
  • Give Each Platform 3-5 Sessions: One session isn't enough to know if it works. Platforms have learning curves, states vary day-to-day. Commit to at least 3-5 sessions before judging.
  • Reassess at 30 Days: After a month: Is this serving you? Are you actually using it? Is the outcome matching the intention? If yes, continue. If no, adjust.
  • Permission to Change: What serves you at one stage may not serve you at the next. Beginners outgrow beginner platforms. Clinical needs shift as healing progresses. Context changes. The right answer now isn't the right answer forever.

The meta-skill: The framework itself becomes invisible with practice. You'll stop consciously running through 'Step 1, Step 2...' and start intuitively matching person to platform. But building that intuition requires starting with the explicit framework.

scenes.narrative:
Three Complex Cases - Full Framework Synthesis

You've learned the four-step framework. Now apply it to three realistic client scenarios. Each scenario presents a complex case with competing factors.

Your task is to identify the recommendation that best balances all four framework steps: Goal, User, Context, and Trial/Iteration. There's one optimal recommendation for each - the others have specific flaws you'll need to spot.

Michael - Chronic Pain + Medicare Coverage

Client Profile: 45, former construction worker on disability due to severe chronic lower back pain (12+ years post-injury). Currently on low-dose pain medication, trying to reduce further. His pain management doctor mentioned VR as an emerging option.

Status: Moderate tech comfort, lives alone in small apartment, very constrained budget (disability income ~$1,800/month), on Medicare. He's skeptical but desperate. No mental health diagnoses. Pain worst in mornings and evenings.

choices.label: A) TRIPP Calm mode + Meta Quest 3S ($300). Affordable entry, stress reduction help.
B) XRHealth clinical pain program + Meta Quest 3 ($500). Insurance coverage, professional oversight.
C) Flowly biofeedback + Meta Quest 3 ($500) + Muse headband ($250). Self-directed control.
D) Nature Treks VR + Meta Quest 3S ($300). Low cost, relaxing environments.

scenes.narrative:
Under-serving

TRIPP is wellness, not clinical pain management. Stress reduction may help pain indirectly, but Michael has access to actual clinical intervention. Recommending wellness when clinical is available and covered is under-serving him.

Financial Barrier

Flowly is evidence-based but requires $250+ in sensors on top of headset, and isn't insurance-covered. For someone on $1,800/month disability income, $750+ upfront investment is prohibitive compared to covered options.

Not Addressing Medical Need

Nature Treks is relaxation, not pain intervention. Recommending this for chronic pain is like recommending a nature walk for a broken leg - pleasant but not addressing the medical need.

Optimal Choice

Why: Michael has a clinical condition with a specific clinical solution available. XRHealth is Medicare-eligible (HCPCS Code E1905), providing licensed therapist supervision remotely. The insurance coverage makes it more accessible despite the higher hardware cost.

Aisha - Facilitator-in-Training, Privacy-Conscious, Germany

Client Profile: 28, Berlin-based yoga teacher training as cyberdelic facilitator. Wants to build practice toolkit - platforms to experience personally and recommend to future clients.

Status: Strong meditation background, ceremonial psychedelic experience. Privacy-conscious (won't use Meta), moderate budget (â‚¬100/month + hardware investment), tech-savvy. Needs platform diversity (wellness, therapeutic, biofeedback, social).

choices.label: A) Meta Quest 3 + TRIPP + Maloka + Healium. Best content access, multiple categories.
B) Pico 4 + GMVR + Open Brush + VRChat meditation communities. Privacy respecting, diverse.
C) Apple Vision Pro + wait for cyberdelic content library to mature. Privacy first.
D) Meta Quest 3 + explain trade-offs between privacy and content access.

scenes.narrative:
Violates Values

Meta Quest violates her non-negotiable privacy requirement. Ignoring a client's core values, even for 'better' content, is dismissive rather than facilitating.

Ineffective for Current Needs

Vision Pro ($3,500) far exceeds reasonable toolkit investment, and 'wait for content' doesn't serve her current training needs. She needs to practice now.

Too Defeatist

This assumes Meta is the only way. Pico exists as a privacy-respecting alternative with a growing library that can fulfill her professional development needs on her terms.

Optimal Choice

Why: It honors her non-negotiable privacy constraint while providing a diverse functional toolkit (Wellness: GMVR, Creative: Open Brush, Social: VRChat). Pico is strong in Europe and doesn't require a Meta account.

Sofia & James - End-of-Life Anxiety

Client Profile: 62, stage IV ovarian cancer (prognosis 12-18 months), significant anticipatory anxiety. Husband James (65) supportive.

Status: Physically weak (fatigues easily), cognitively sharp, emotionally open. Interested in anxiety reduction and existential questions. Neither is tech-savvy, financially comfortable. Sofia is in palliative care with a therapist.

choices.label: A) Nature Treks VR + Meta Quest 3S + James helps. Gentle, low cognitive load, short sessions.
B) Maloka facilitated sessions + Meta Quest 3. Addresses existential depth, professional container.
C) TRIPP Calm mode + Meta Quest 3. Evidence-based, James learns to guide daily practice.
D) The Blu + Meta Quest 3S. Very short sessions (5 min) for awe induction.

scenes.narrative:
Physically Overwhelming

Maloka is high-intensity work requiring significant energy and processing capacity. Asking a physically weak patient to engage in long preparation/journey sessions is inappropriate for her current capacity.

Complexity Mismatch

TRIPP's gamification (streaks, progress) is for long-term practice building. Also, requiring James to 'learn to guide' adds unnecessary learning curve compared to simple nature immersion.

Too Short / Higher Cognitive Load

5 minutes is often too short to establish calm. Paging through different experiences in The Blu creates more cognitive load than the consistent, gentle environments of Nature Treks.

Optimal Choice

Why: Requires medical sensitivity, accessibility, and immediate utility. Nature Treks is simple, calming, and adjustable in duration - widely used in palliative care. It addresses anxiety without overwhelming her physical capacity.

Scenario Complete

Notice how the 'correct' answer wasn't about the 'best' platform in abstractâ€”it was about matching the specific person's goals, constraints, context, and capacity.

Michael needed clinical/insurance coverage. Aisha needed privacy-respecting diversity. Sofia needed gentle immediate accessibility.

sections.title: Organizing Your Knowledge
sections.content:
Before you move forward, let's make sure the entire cyberdelic landscape you've just toured is organized clearly in your mind. You've covered five lessons of platforms, applications, clinical evidence, biofeedback technology, social VR, hardware, and selection frameworks - and it's a lot.

The exercise below is your chance to demonstrate that you haven't just learned facts, but that you can organize the landscape the way an expert would. Once you can place every major platform in its right category without hesitation, you're ready for Module 5 - where we zoom out from 'what exists' to 'who builds it and how.'

sections.title: Summary & Transition
sections.content:
Excellent work. You've just organized the Module 4 landscape the way an expert would. You've built a mental model of how platforms relate to categories and to each other.

Notice that the field isn't fixed boxes; it's a spectrum. You can now place any new platform you encounter by asking: What's its core purpose? Who does it serve? What evidence backs it?

In Module 4, you learned what exists and how to navigate it.

In Module 5, you'll learn who builds all of this, how the field sustains itself, and where you might fit within it.

The applications you just mapped are made possible by a broader ecosystem - researchers, investors, facilitators, regulators, and communities all working in relationship. Let's explore the system that makes the landscape possible.

questions.question:
Trial & Iterate

In the four-step selection framework, why is 'Trial & Iterate' the final step rather than 'Choose the Perfect Platform'?

options.label: Because no platform is perfect so expectations should be lowered.
Because what serves someone changes over time, and trial reveals fit better than analysis alone.
Because platforms frequently shut down so users need backup options.
Because it's impossible to know what someone needs without trying everything.

synthesis.q1_a: This is too pessimistic. The point is that the right platform for someone evolves as their practice deepens.
synthesis.q1_b: Correct! Beginners outgrow beginner platforms, and clinical needs shift as healing progresses. The right answer now isn't the right answer forever.
synthesis.q1_c: Stability is a factor, but not the core reason for iteration in facilitation.
synthesis.q1_d: Trial & Iterate doesn't mean random testing; it means informed starting choices followed by refinement.

questions.question:
Assessing Readiness

A client says: 'I want the most powerful VR consciousness experience available - whatever produces the deepest altered states.' What's your most important first response?

options.label: Ask about their hoped-for outcome and support systems.
Discuss privacy concerns vs. content access.

synthesis.q2_a: Budget matters eventually, but safety and readiness come first.
synthesis.q2_b: Tech skills are practical, but psychological readiness for intensity is more critical.
synthesis.q2_c: Correct! This explores the underlying need (what are they seeking?) and assesses readiness (do they have preparation and support?). High-intensity work requires a container, not just an app.
synthesis.q2_d: Privacy is important, but secondary to the safety implications of altered-state requests.

content.title: The Cyberdelic Landscape Map
content.instruction: Drag each platform into its primary category - based on core design purpose and primary use case.
